P.04
KlisterFixer
Applicator
2026
CU Boulder GEEN 2400
Demo Day: GEEN 2400 ExpoThe full KlisterFixer team presenting at the Engineering Projects for the Community expo. Left to right: Molly More, Kristina Morgunova, Aiden Bunas, Cooper Smith, and Dinuka Hannadige.
Bench ValidationTesting the heating circuit with an Agilent multimeter and Keysight power supply, confirming the 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and sizing the LiPo battery for 1-hour field use.
Full PrototypeFinal assembled KlisterFixer in purple PLA showing the complete form: klister tube chamber, sliding cover, handle, and trigger arm. This prototype was used for field testing and the demo day presentation.
Design Iteration SketchesHand-drawn sketches with dimensions and a future fixes list covering trigger geometry, cover design, and LED slot placement. These fed directly into the next printed revision.
Field Test ResultEven klister coverage on the ski base after using KlisterFixer outdoors with the CU Nordic Ski Club. Application time cut from 15-30 minutes to under 10 minutes, with no direct hand contact.
Electronics & Systems Integration

Designed and built for CU Boulder's GEEN 2400 Engineering Projects for the Community course, in partnership with the CU Nordic Ski Club. Klister wax is essential for cross-country skiing in icy conditions, but applying it outdoors in the cold with ski gloves on is a mess. KlisterFixer is a handheld battery-powered tool that heats klister to 35-40°C, lets you squeeze it onto the ski with a trigger, and lights up when it hits the right temperature. No direct hand contact with the wax.

  • Designed and iterated the temperature-sensing indicator circuit using an Arduino Nano, transistor-switched resistive heating element, and dual LED feedback (green = ready, yellow = warming) to signal when optimal klister application temperature was reached.
  • Bench-validated heating element performance with a Keysight programmable power supply and Agilent multimeter, confirming 11.3V / 1.13A / 12.8W operating point and sizing a 1500 mAh LiPo battery for 1-hour field use.
  • Contributed to the iterative design process through sketches, design reviews, and hands-on prototype feedback, pushing improvements to trigger geometry, LED slot placement, and housing ergonomics across multiple printed revisions.
  • Supported prototype assembly, integrating LiPo battery, PCB, and wiring harness into the 3D-printed housing and foam-backed grip module across successive iterations.
  • Participated in outdoor field trials with the Nordic Ski Club, confirming the device works with ski gloves and cuts application time from 15-30 minutes down to under 10 minutes per person.
